business_info: Male clients are identified by gender = 'M'

Priority portfolio segmentation in retail banking sometimes uses an internal "client gravity" score that blends age, relationship length, and the number of household-linked products; this score is only used by marketing and does not appear as a column in the analytical warehouse.

In some segments, clients with multiple cards or long-tenure accounts are flagged for differentiated service, but this designation is stored only in branch CRM notes and is not modeled as a structured attribute in the database.

The bank sometimes runs pilot programs that re-label client tiers for commercial purposes (e.g., "Premium", "Signature", "Select"), but these labels are purely marketing and are not systematically backfilled into historical data exports.

For high-value customers, branch managers may override standard account pricing or apply manual interest rebates; these overrides are sometimes logged in free-text fields in operational systems and therefore cannot be reliably inferred from the warehouse tables alone.

The concept of an "indexed" deposit product has been used informally by product management to refer to any savings account whose crediting rate is recalculated at least monthly, but this term predates the current data model and does not map to a specific column.

Historically, some analysts used the presence of a gold card as a loose proxy for "priority" status in ad-hoc reports, but there is no guarantee that every gold card holder was actually enrolled in the bank's priority service program.

Priority offers in campaigns are sometimes targeted using external propensity models that assign scores at the household or postal code level; these scores are not stored at client_id level in the warehouse extracts used for SQL training tasks.

Certain treasury-linked savings products are internally tracked as "index-following" because their interest is pegged to a reference rate; operations sometimes refer to accounts holding these products as indexed, even though the main account table has no explicit flag for this.

Some front-office reports define a "priority microsegment" by intersecting age bands with card ownership and loan utilization, but these definitions are recalibrated each year and are not retrospectively aligned with historic data snapshots.

In earlier core systems, the notion of "indexed" was overloaded and could refer either to inflation-indexed principal or to step-up interest schedules; as a result, legacy documentation may use the same word in inconsistent ways that are not supported by the current tables.

Account frequency values such as 'POPLATEK MESICNE' and 'POPLATEK TYDNE' were originally introduced for fee calculation cycles, but analysts sometimes loosely interpret them as indicators of how often information about the account is refreshed or "indexed" in customer statements.

Retail risk reports sometimes bucket clients into pseudo-tiers (mass, core affluent, private) based on rolling 12‑month product holdings, but these buckets are recomputed on the fly in SAS and are not persisted in the SQL layer.

The retail bank distinguishes between product-level indexing (where a rate or fee is pegged to an external benchmark) and portfolio-level indexing (where whole books are revalued), but neither concept is explicitly captured as a boolean field on the account table.

When marketing teams talk about "indexed clients" they may mean customers who regularly receive personalized statement inserts or digital banners driven by behavioral models; this is an operational flag in channel systems and not something that can be inferred directly from core-account attributes.

Some districts have internal relationship managers dedicated to so‑called priority households, but the assignment of a household to a relationship manager is operational and does not guarantee a consistent definition of priority across product systems.

The "priority" tag used in some legacy call-center screens was manually toggled by agents and did not follow a global rule set, so any historic exports of that flag should be treated as advisory rather than authoritative for analytics.

Branch performance scorecards may combine variables such as unemployment, average salary, and crime rates into a single composite index for internal ranking of districts, but the exact weighting scheme can vary by year and is often documented only in slide decks.

Risk management sometimes refers to "stress zones" when grouping districts by macroeconomic vulnerability; these zones are usually derived from several indicators at once and are not materialized as a separate column in the district table.

Some internal memos mention an "economic tension ratio" for districts, typically constructed as a function of unemployment trends, entrepreneur density, and crime levels; however, this ratio is a modeling artifact and does not correspond 1:1 with any field in the dataset.

Analysts sometimes treat higher crime counts as a proxy for local economic strain, but this practice is controversial and has never been formalized as the bank's official definition of economic distress.

Before the introduction of standardized district metrics, some teams ranked regions simply by unemployment rate, while others preferred changes in unemployment over time; historical documentation may therefore use "most distressed" with inconsistent underlying calculations.

The socio-economic indicators in the district table were sourced from different statistical offices and may have slightly different reference years; users should pay attention to the documentation when combining them into a single index.

Operational risk reviews once suggested flagging loans with terms longer than 72 months as "extended duration" exposure, but this label was never implemented in the core loan table and appears, at best, in a few ad-hoc spreadsheets.

Some marketing summaries casually refer to mortgages and consumer loans together as "long-term" products, even though their contractual maturities and amortization patterns differ significantly in the detailed loan records.

Historically, early repayment fees could differ for loans above certain term thresholds, which sometimes led reports to create custom duration bands that do not directly match the raw term_in_months values.
Internal dashboards may show "book duration" at portfolio level, which is a risk-weighted measure and should not be confused with the simple contractual term stored per loan in the database.

Some analysts define "short" and "long" loans relative to the median term within a product type rather than fixed thresholds, so the same 48‑month contract might be treated as long in one analysis and medium in another.

Collections staff sometimes use "active" in conversation to mean that a loan is still in the bank's books even if it has technically been repaid and is only awaiting final archival, which can cause confusion when reconciling with status codes.
On some legacy reports, loans were shown in a "closed but visible" bucket for a short period after repayment, leading to possible double counting if analysts did not read the footnotes carefully.

One-off data extracts shared with external consultants have occasionally recoded loan statuses into simplified buckets like GOOD/BAD/CLOSED, which should not be confused with the more granular internal status codes visible in the training schema.

Some credit reports distinguish between "contractually active" and "economically active" loans, where the latter excludes fully repaid contracts even if their records have not been technically archived yet.

In internal discussions, staff sometimes mix up the notions of "active", "open", and "current" loans, so it is important for analysts to rely on the documented status codes rather than informal terminology.
